author: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

In this book Augustin refutes the second letter which Petilianus wrote to him after having seen the first of Augustin’s earlier books. This letter had been full of violent language; and Augustin rather shows that the arguments of Petilianus had been deficient and irrelevant, than brings forward arguments in support of his own statements.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But this is not what we are now inquiring.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let him rather answer (what he wanders off into the most irrelevant matters in order to avoid answering) by what means the conscience of the recipient is cleansed who is unacquainted with the stain on the conscience of the giver, if the conscience of one that gives in holiness is what we look for to cleanse the conscience of the recipient? and from what source he receive faith who is unwittingly baptized by one that is faithless, if he that has wittingly received his faith from one that is faithless receives not faith but guilt?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Omitting, therefore, his revilings, which he has cast at me without any sound consideration, let us still notice that he does not say what we demand in what follows.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But I should like to look at the garrulous mode in which he has set this forth, as though he were sure to overwhelm us with confusion.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: "But let us return," he says, "to that argument of your fancy, whereby you seem to have represented to yourself in a form of words the persons you baptize.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For since you do not see the truth, it would have been more seemly to have imagined what was probable."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: These words of his own, Petilianus put forth by way of preface, being about to state the words that I had used.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Then he went on to quote: "Behold, you say, the faithless man stands ready to baptize, but he who is to be baptized knows nothing of his faithlessness."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He has not quoted the whole of my proposition and question; and presently he begins to ask me in his turn, saying, "Who is the man, and from what corner has he started up, that you propose to us?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why do you seem to see a man who is the produce of your imagination, in order to avoid seeing one whom you are bound to see, and to examine and test most carefully?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But since I see that you are unacquainted with the order of the sacrament, I tell you this as shortly as I can: you were bound both to examine your baptizer, and to be examined by him."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What is it, then, that we were waiting for?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: That he should tell us by what means the conscience of the recipient is to be cleansed, who is unacquainted with the stain on the conscience of him that gives but not in holiness, and whence the man is to receive not guilt but faith, who has received baptism unwittingly from one that is faithless.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: All that we have heard is that the baptizer ought most diligently to be examined by him who wishes to receive not guilt but faith, that the latter may make himself acquainted with the conscience of him that gives in holiness, which is to cleanse the conscience of the recipient.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For the man that has failed to make this examination, and has unwittingly received baptism from one that is faithless, from the very fact that he did not make the examination, and therefore did not know of the stain on the conscience of the giver, was incapacitated from receiving faith instead of guilt.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why therefore did he add what he made so much of adding,—the word wittingly, which he calumniously accused me of having suppressed?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For in his unwillingness that the sentence should run, "He who has received his faith from one that is faithless, receives not faith but guilt," he seems to have left some hope to the man that acts unwittingly.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But now, when he is asked whence that man is to receive faith who is baptized unwittingly by one that is faithless, he has answered that he ought to have examined his baptizer; so that, beyond all doubt, he refuses the wretched man permission even to be ignorant, by not finding out from what source he may receive faith, unless he has placed his trust in the man that is baptizing him.
